message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN. Double-check before saving.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[zugzwang]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path and the word count.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Master Roadmap Flowchart: 1500 to 2800
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: A single large Mermaid flowchart (flowchart TD) showing the whole journey: rating bands as stages, skills unlocked at each, and the feedback loop play-analyze-train-weakness. Add a short legend and links to each band roadmap file. This file is mostly the flowchart plus brief notes; the standard 11-section template does NOT apply.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/flowcharts/master-roadmap-flowchart.md

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- artifact-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use when building web components, pages, or applications. Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll write the master roadmap flowchart file. This is the flowchart-focused variant, so I'll build one large Mermaid diagram plus a legend and band links.

input.content:
# Master Roadmap Flowchart: 1500 to 2800 > The whole journey on one page: which rating bands you pass through, the concrete skills each band unlocks, and the play-analyze-train feedback loop that drives the climb. **Rating focus:** 1500–2800 FIDE (full curriculum overview) **Prerequisites:** none — start here, then branch into each band roadmap ## How to read this map This file is the spine of the curriculum. The rest of the material lives in per-band roadmap files; this page shows how they connect and the order to attack them. Unlike the topic files, it does not follow the 11-section template — it is the flowchart plus short notes and links. Three things are happening in the diagram below: 1. **Stages (the vertical spine).** Each box is a rating band. You climb roughly bottom-to-top, and you do not skip — a 1700 trying to study 2400 endgame theory is wasting reps. Master the unlock skills of your band before reaching for the next. 2. **Unlocked skills (the branches).** Each band lists the 2–4 capabilities that *define* graduating from it. These are not "topics you have heard of" — they are things you can do reliably under a clock. 3. **The feedback loop (the engine).** Off to the side sits the cycle that actually moves your number: **Play → Analyze → Find weakness → Train it → Play again.** No band is cleared by reading alone. The loop is what converts study into rating. A blunt truth from the coaching chair: 90% of players under 2000 lose rated games to one-move tactical oversights and basic endgame ignorance, not to deep opening theory. Spend your time accordingly — the diagram weights early bands toward calculation, tactics, and technique, and only loads heavy theory in the upper stages. ## The master flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start your journey]) --> B1500 subgraph SPINE [Rating bands - climb bottom to top] direction TB B1500[1500 to 1700
Consolidate basics] --> B1700[1700 to 1900
Convert advantages] B1700 --> B1900[1900 to 2100
Positional play] B1900 --> B2100[2100 to 2300
Candidate Master craft] B2100 --> B2300[2300 to 2500
FIDE Master to IM] B2300 --> B2500[2500 to 2800
GM and elite] end B1500 -.unlocks.-> S1[No one move blunders
Basic mates
Pins forks skewers] B1700 -.unlocks.-> S2[Calculation 2 to 3 ply clean
King and pawn endings
Opening principles] B1900 -.unlocks.-> S3[Pawn structures
Good vs bad pieces
Prophylaxis] B2100 -.unlocks.-> S4[Deep calculation
Rook endgames
A real repertoire] B2300 -.unlocks.-> S5[Dynamic sacrifice
Theoretical endgames
Prep with engines] B2500 -.unlocks.-> S6[Universality
Practical decisions
Self directed prep] B2500 --> Goal([2800 elite practical strength]) subgraph LOOP [The engine - run every week at every band] direction LR Play[Play slow games] --> Analyze[Analyze yourself first
then engine] Analyze --> Weakness[Name one weakness] Weakness --> Train[Train that weakness
puzzles drills theory] Train --> Play end LOOP -. drives every stage .-> SPINE ``` ## Legend - **Rounded boxes** `([ ])` — the entry and the 2800 goal: where you begin and the practical ceiling this curriculum targets. - **Rectangles in SPINE** — rating bands. Solid arrows `-->` show the only legal climb order: clear a band's unlock skills before moving up. - **Dotted "unlocks" arrows** `-.->` — the defining skills you must own to graduate that band. If you cannot do them under a clock, you have not really left the band, whatever your number says this week. - **LOOP subgraph** — the weekly engine. It is drawn once but applies at *every* stage; the dotted "drives every stage" arrow means you never stop running it. - **Reading direction** — `flowchart TD`: the spine reads top-down as boxes but you *climb* upward in strength. Lower boxes = earlier work. ## The feedback loop in plain words The loop is the single most important idea on this page, so here it is without the diagram: 1. **Play slow games.** Classical or rapid with real think time (15+10 minimum). Blitz teaches patterns you already know; it does not build new ones. Aim for 2–4 serious games a week. 2. **Analyze yourself first.** Before turning on the engine, write down where you think the game turned and what you were thinking. *Then* check with the engine. The gap between your assessment and the truth is your curriculum. 3. **Name one weakness.** Not five. One. "I hang pieces when attacked on the queenside." "I don't know the Lucena position." Specificity is everything. 4. **Train that weakness.** Targeted puzzles (Lichess themes), a chapter from the relevant band file, or 20 reps of a drill. Match the training to the named weakness, not to whatever is fun. 5. **Play again.** Test whether the fix held. If yes, name the next weakness. If no, train the same one harder. This is the whole game. ## What each band really demands A few coach's notes the boxes are too small to hold: - **1500–1700.** Stop hanging pieces. That is 80% of the work. Run a blunder-check before every move: "Is anything of mine attacked? What does my opponent threaten?" Know the three basic mates (Q+K, R+K, two rooks) cold. - **1700–1900.** You can see tactics; now *convert*. Win won positions. This means clean king-and-pawn endings (opposition, the rule of the square, key squares) and not throwing away material under pressure. - **1900–2100.** The jump to *understanding* rather than just calculating. Pawn structures dictate plans; you learn good vs. bad bishops, weak squares, and prophylaxis — asking what your opponent wants and stopping it. - **2100–2300.** Candidate Master craft: deeper, cleaner calculation; rook endgames (Lucena, Philidor) as second nature; and a genuine, maintained opening repertoire rather than a pile of memorized lines. - **2300–2500.** Dynamics — sound sacrifices for initiative, theoretical endgame precision, and serious engine-assisted preparation tailored to opponents. - **2500–2800.** Universality and practical strength: handle any structure, make good decisions under fatigue and time pressure, and direct your own preparation without a coach holding the wheel. ## Band roadmaps Each band has its own roadmap file. Start at your current strength, not your aspirational one. - [1500–1700 roadmap](../roadmaps/band-1500-1700.md) - [1700–1900 roadmap](../roadmaps/band-1700-1900.md) - [1900–2100 roadmap](../roadmaps/band-1900-2100.md) - [2100–2300 roadmap](../roadmaps/band-2100-2300.md) - [2300–2500 roadmap](../roadmaps/band-2300-2500.md) - [2500–2800 roadmap](../roadmaps/band-2500-2800.md) ## Where to run the loop - **Play:** classical/rapid on [Lichess](https://lichess.org) or over the board in a club. Avoid living in bullet. - **Analyze:** the [Lichess analysis board](https://lichess.org/analysis) — your own assessment first, engine second. - **Train:** [Lichess puzzles by theme](https://lichess.org/training/themes) for tactics; the band files for structured study; Dvoretsky's *Endgame Manual* and Aagaard's *Calculation* once you reach the upper bands. ## Related - [How to spot tactics](../tactics/how-to-spot-tactics.md) · [[calculation]] - [King and pawn endgames](../endgames/king-and-pawn.md) · [[opposition]] - [Prophylaxis](../strategy/prophylaxis.md) · [[prophylaxis]] - [Zugzwang](../endgames/zugzwang.md) · [[zugzwang]] - [Building a repertoire](../openings/building-a-repertoire.md) · [[repertoire]]
